assumptions: Traffic assumed ~<50k MAU (sensible scalable default — not stated)
WebSocket connections are persistent; Lambda cold-start latency makes it unsuitable as the primary connection handler
Chat messages are stored persistently (not ephemeral); DynamoDB is the primary message/session store
Presence and recent-message caching via ElastiCache Redis
Single AWS region unless stated otherwise
No compliance regime specified — standard security floor applied
Pub/sub fan-out for message delivery uses Redis Pub/Sub on the same ElastiCache cluster, upgraded to ElastiCache + SQS fan-out at resilient tier
Budget tier uses a single EC2 box running Docker Compose (WebSocket server + self-managed Redis) — serverless-no-VPC does not fit persistent WebSocket connections at budget scale
Alerting delivered to email at budget, Slack at balanced, PagerDuty at resilient
Cost estimates are on-demand list prices for us-east-1, presented as ranges over assumed monthly volumes. They exclude the AWS Free Tier, Savings Plans, Reserved Instances, and negotiated discounts.
Assumed traffic ~100k–1M requests/month, applied equally to all three tiers (tiers differ by robustness, not traffic). Set "expected monthly visitors" in intake to change it.

tiers.name: budget
tiers.summary: Single EC2 (t4g.small) in a public subnet running Docker Compose: Node.js/Go WebSocket server + self-managed Redis on localhost. DynamoDB on-demand for persistent messages and sessions. CloudFront + WAF at the edge. No ALB, no NAT gateway, no separate managed cache.
delta: Single EC2 (t4g.small) public subnet — no ALB, no NAT, no separate managed cache (~$0 egress overhead)
Self-managed Redis on localhost for pub/sub fan-out and presence (no ElastiCache cost)
DynamoDB on-demand absorbs write bursts without provisioning
CloudFront + WAF at edge — WebSocket proxy via CloudFront (WSS passthrough)
CloudWatch Logs + key alarms + SNS email alert; self-managed backup via nightly pg_dump-style DynamoDB export to S3
Single AZ — no HA; instance failure requires manual or auto-recovery
tradeoffs: Single point of failure: instance crash = full outage until EC2 auto-recovery (~1–3 min)
Self-managed Redis: no replication, no persistence (presence/pub-sub state lost on restart)
No horizontal scale — all connections on one box; vertical resize requires restart
CloudFront WebSocket proxying adds one hop but avoids NLB cost at this scale

costDrivers.note: Data transfer out to internet, first 10TB/month tier (North America). First 1TB/month is free.
HTTPS requests ($0.0075 per 10,000). North America/Europe.
Assumed-throughput estimate: t4g.small on-demand (us-east-1). Cost = instance count × running hours; storage/IO billed separately, multi-AZ via the tier multiplier.
On-demand read request units ($0.125 per 1M RRU). 1 RRU = 1 strongly-consistent read up to 4KB.
On-demand write request units ($0.625 per 1M WRU). 1 WRU = 1 write up to 1KB.
S3 Standard storage, first 50TB/month tier.
PUT/COPY/POST/LIST requests per 1,000. GET/SELECT requests are ~$0.0004 per 1,000.
CloudWatch Logs ingestion $0.50/GB (first 10GB/mo free in some accounts). Archival storage is cheaper; Insights queries extra.
Standard topic API requests ($0.50 per 1M). Delivery (HTTP/SMS/email) priced separately.

tiers.name: balanced
tiers.summary: Multi-AZ Fargate service behind an NLB. Managed ElastiCache Redis cluster for pub/sub fan-out and presence. DynamoDB unchanged. Private subnets for Fargate tasks and ElastiCache. NAT Gateway for outbound. CloudWatch dashboards + X-Ray tracing.

edges.payload: outbound AWS API calls (private subnet egress)

delta: Single EC2 → Fargate multi-AZ tasks in private subnets (ECS service auto-scaling on CPU + connection count)
Self-managed Redis → ElastiCache Redis multi-AZ replica cluster (managed failover ~60s)
Direct EC2 public IP → NLB (TCP passthrough, sticky target groups for persistent connections)
Added NAT Gateway for private-subnet outbound egress (~$33/mo always-on cost)
Added X-Ray tracing across Fargate ↔ DynamoDB ↔ ElastiCache boundaries
SNS alert upgraded to email + Slack webhook on critical alarms
ECS task auto-scaling absorbs burst; ElastiCache absorbs hot read/pub-sub bursts
tradeoffs: NAT Gateway is an always-on cost even at zero traffic
Single NAT GW = AZ failure risk for outbound; resilient tier adds per-AZ NAT
ElastiCache failover ~60s — brief pub/sub disruption on primary failure
NLB adds per-LCU cost vs direct EC2 IP at budget

S3 Standard storage, first 50TB/month tier.
PUT/COPY/POST/LIST requests per 1,000. GET/SELECT requests are ~$0.0004 per 1,000.
CloudWatch Logs ingestion $0.50/GB (first 10GB/mo free in some accounts). Archival storage is cheaper; Insights queries extra.

Assumed-throughput estimate: per GB memory-hour (Linux/x86). Total = memoryGB x hours.
Assumed-throughput estimate: per vCPU-hour (Linux/x86). Total = vCPU x hours.
Assumed-throughput estimate: cache.t4g.small on-demand (us-east-1). Cost = instance count × running hours; storage/IO billed separately, multi-AZ via the tier multiplier.
Per GB processed. Forced by the private-subnet data-tier default (security baseline no-public-data-tier); the most common budget surprise.
Per NAT gateway-hour (always-on, ~$32.40/month per AZ before data processing).
X-Ray traces: $5.00 per 1M traces recorded (first 100k/mo free).
Required by the private-subnet security default (no public data tier) — the most common budget surprise. Inter-AZ data transfer, charged $0.01/GB in each direction.
Required by the private-subnet security default (no public data tier) — the most common budget surprise. Data transfer out to internet, first 10TB/month tier. First 100GB/month is free.

tiers.name: resilient
tiers.summary: Multi-AZ Fargate with per-AZ NAT Gateways. ElastiCache Redis Global Datastore for cross-region pub/sub durability. DynamoDB Global Tables for active-active message replication. SQS per-room message fan-out queue with DLQ. Route 53 health-checked failover. CloudWatch anomaly detection + PagerDuty escalation.

edges.payload: outbound AWS API calls (private subnet egress)
DNS A record with health-check failover
fan-out message delivery to subscriber tasks

delta: Single NAT GW → one NAT Gateway per AZ (eliminates cross-AZ egress failure blast radius)
ElastiCache single-region → Global Datastore (cross-region pub/sub replication)
DynamoDB on-demand → Global Tables (active-active multi-region message replication + PITR)
Added Route 53 health-checked DNS failover across regions
Added SQS FIFO fan-out queue per room with DLQ + idempotent consumer (guaranteed delivery over pure Redis Pub/Sub)
Added EventBridge bus for room/presence lifecycle events enabling future fan-out consumers
Fargate min warm task count set to absorb sub-minute spikes auto-scaling cannot catch
CloudWatch upgraded to anomaly detection + SLO burn-rate alarms; SNS → PagerDuty escalation
tradeoffs: Per-AZ NAT Gateways increase fixed egress cost proportionally to AZ count
Global Tables + Global Datastore add significant replication cost vs single-region
SQS FIFO fan-out introduces ~ms delivery latency vs pure in-process Redis Pub/Sub
Most complex operational surface — justified only at mission-critical / multi-region DR requirement

keyDecisions.decision: Compute model for WebSocket connection tier
keyDecisions.chosen: Always-on container (Fargate at balanced/resilient; single EC2 at budget)
alternativesConsidered: Lambda WebSocket via API Gateway WebSocket API
keyDecisions.rationale: Persistent TCP connections require always-on, long-lived processes; Lambda's execution model and cold-start latency break real-time chat semantics (Performance Efficiency + Reliability).

keyDecisions.decision: Budget tier shape — single box vs managed stack
keyDecisions.chosen: Single EC2 (t4g.small) running Docker Compose: WebSocket server + self-managed Redis bound to localhost
alternativesConsidered: Lambda + API Gateway WebSocket API + DynamoDB
keyDecisions.rationale: At budget scale a single public-subnet EC2 eliminates the ~$100+/mo always-on NAT + ALB + Fargate + ElastiCache floor while preserving the correct persistent-connection topology (Cost Optimization).

keyDecisions.chosen: Redis Pub/Sub (budget/balanced) → SQS per-room queue fan-out (resilient)
keyDecisions.rationale: Redis Pub/Sub is already in the stack and gives sub-ms in-process fan-out; SQS fan-out added only at resilient tier where multi-region durability and DLQ guarantees matter (Reliability + Cost Optimization).

DynamoDB on-demand absorbs bursty write spikes without provisioning, scales horizontally, and needs no VPC, keeping the data tier simple and eliminating NAT at budget (Performance Efficiency + Cost Optimization).

keyDecisions.chosen: NLB (Network Load Balancer) at balanced/resilient — NOT ALB
keyDecisions.rationale: NLB passes TCP/TLS directly to Fargate tasks preserving persistent WebSocket connections without HTTP-layer rewriting; ALB HTTP/2 layer can interfere with long-lived upgrade flows (Reliability + Performance Efficiency).

keyDecisions.chosen: ElastiCache Redis (Valkey/Redis-compatible) — separate cluster at balanced/resilient, self-managed on-box at budget
keyDecisions.rationale: Redis delivers sub-ms presence lookups and pub/sub fan-out; in-process memory breaks with multiple Fargate tasks, and DynamoDB TTL has second-level granularity too coarse for real-time presence (Performance Efficiency).

securityFloor: Encrypt all data at rest (KMS / service-native SSE), preferring customer-managed keys.
Enforce TLS in transit everywhere; terminate HTTPS at the edge and reject plaintext.
Least-privilege IAM: scoped per-service roles, no wildcards, no long-lived access keys.
Enable S3 Block Public Access account-wide so no bucket or object can be made public.
Keep the data tier private: VPC-bound stores in private subnets with no public route; managed/serverless stores (DynamoDB/S3) are already private via IAM and need no VPC or NAT.
Store secrets in AWS Secrets Manager / SSM Parameter Store — never in code or images.
Front public endpoints with CloudFront + AWS WAF (managed + rate-based rules); Shield Standard for L3/L4.
Enable CloudTrail plus S3/CloudFront/ALB access logs and VPC Flow Logs for audit.
